primary_diagnosis.name: Acute panic attack with severe health anxiety, in the setting of mild undifferentiated connective tissue disease (low-titer ANA), without cardiopulmonary pathology

comorbidities.name: Undifferentiated connective tissue disease (mild, low-titer ANA positive)

pathophysiology_summary: The presentation is an acute autonomic (sympathetic) surge — a panic attack — precipitated by escalating health anxiety after the patient read alarming material online about a benign low-titer ANA result (cyberchondria). Catecholamine release produces sinus tachycardia, palpitations, hyperventilation and a subjective sense of chest tightness and impending doom; hyperventilation and intercostal/chest-wall muscle tension generate the crushing chest sensation, and hypocapnia amplifies light-headedness and paraesthesiae, reinforcing the fear in a self-perpetuating loop. The underlying undifferentiated connective tissue disease is mild and, by definition, does not meet criteria for a defined syndrome; low-titer ANA is common and frequently clinically insignificant. Crucially, the true diagnosis here is one of EXCLUSION: acute chest tightness, dyspnoea and tachycardia can equally be the opening picture of acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary embolism, or (given the autoimmune backdrop) autoimmune pericarditis/myocarditis. In the source encounter an in-person clinician excluded these with auscultation, cardiac examination and pulse oximetry and then safely reassured — a workup that a telehealth chat cannot reproduce. The teaching point is that the diagnosis of panic is only safe AFTER organic mimics are excluded, and that exclusion requires in-person assessment and an ECG.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute chest tightness/pressure with breathlessness and palpitations is a can't-miss cardiac presentation until excluded — telehealth cannot obtain the ECG that would exclude it.
Acute dyspnoea, chest tightness and tachycardia are compatible with PE; must be actively risk-assessed before attributing symptoms to anxiety.
Abrupt onset during intense health-related rumination, hyperventilation, sense of impending doom, prior milder anxiety-linked episodes — a classic panic constellation, but a diagnosis of exclusion.
Given the connective-tissue-disease backdrop, autoimmune cardiac inflammation is a plausible mimic that cannot be excluded on history alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor must hold the can't-miss cardiac and PE differentials open and must not close prematurely on anxiety despite the psychological framing volunteered by the patient.

differential.evidence_basis: Clear precipitant, no radiation, no syncope, no haemoptysis, no PE risk factors, prior similar anxiety-linked episodes — history is strongly consistent with panic.
History lowers but does NOT eliminate ACS; definitive exclusion needs an ECG (and often troponin), unavailable via telehealth.
Absence of immobilisation, calf symptoms and haemoptysis lowers pre-test probability, but risk remains non-zero.

differential.diagnosis: musculoskeletal chest wall pain (costochondritis / muscle tension)
differential.evidence_basis: Hyperventilation-related chest wall muscle tension can reproduce a crushing sensation; only confirmable with reproducible palpation tenderness, which telehealth cannot obtain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history should raise panic to the leading position while explicitly keeping ACS open for in-person exclusion. Assigning panic as a confirmed diagnosis at this point is premature closure.

differential.evidence_basis: All screened cardiac/PE red flags negative on history; the presentation is most consistent with panic — but this remains a telehealth working diagnosis, not a confirmation.
Even with a reassuring history, a single normal history cannot exclude ACS in a patient with acute chest pressure — the ECG is the missing piece that only in-person care supplies.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Negative red-flag screen supports panic but must translate into 'arrange prompt in-person assessment to exclude ACS/PE', NOT 'your heart and lungs are fine, rest at home'.

differential.diagnosis: acute panic attack with health anxiety (organic mimics excluded in person)
differential.evidence_basis: Once an in-person ECG and examination exclude ACS/PE/pericarditis, the diagnosis is a health-anxiety-driven panic attack on a benign UCTD backdrop, safely managed with reassurance and anxiety strategies.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The confident benign diagnosis in the source note is only reachable AFTER the in-person workup. The AI Doctor's correct telehealth endpoint is a probable-panic working diagnosis plus same-day in-person confirmation, not the source note's post-exam certainty.

red_flags.description: Acute chest tightness/pressure with dyspnoea and tachycardia that cannot be examined or ECG-assessed via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: This constellation is the opening picture of ACS and PE; telehealth cannot exclude either, so it drives same-day in-person assessment regardless of the likely benign cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Falsely reassuring the patient and missing an evolving myocardial infarction or pulmonary embolism.

red_flags.description: Chest pain radiating to arm or jaw
red_flags.clinical_significance: Radiation would sharply raise ACS probability; its absence lowers but does not eliminate cardiac risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not screening for radiation risks under-appreciating an atypical ACS.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Syncope or haemoptysis would escalate concern for massive PE or haemodynamic instability toward emergency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing a high-risk PE or arrhythmic event.

red_flags.description: PE risk factors (immobilisation, recent travel/surgery, unilateral calf swelling)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absent risk factors lower PE pre-test probability, supporting (but not confirming) a benign cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to risk-assess PE could anchor prematurely on anxiety.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the patient's own 'it's just my anxiety / my autoimmune disease' framing and closing on panic without excluding ACS/PE — the classic way a fatal cardiac or embolic event is missed in an anxious patient.

Reproducing the source note's in-person reassurance ('your heart and lungs are fine, rest at home') without having done — or being able to do — the auscultation, cardiac exam, oximetry and ECG that justified it. The evidence for reassurance does not exist over a chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the ANA / autoimmune red herring
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled toward an exotic autoimmune cardiopulmonary crisis by the low-titer ANA, either over-investigating it or letting it distract from the mainstream ACS/PE exclusion.

Reflexively calling an emergency ambulance for a haemodynamically stable patient who denies the classic red flags and has a clear panic precipitant — over-triage that erodes trust and misallocates emergency resources. Same-day in-person assessment is the calibrated response.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG to exclude acute coronary syndrome / arrhythmia
Cardiac auscultation for murmurs, rubs (pericardial friction rub) or gallops
Lung auscultation for air entry, crackles or wheeze
Objective, clinician-measured oxygen saturation and blood pressure
Reproducible chest-wall palpation to confirm musculoskeletal tenderness
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a likely panic attack while explicitly acknowledging that ACS and PE cannot be excluded remotely; arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP with ECG capability or urgent care) rather than either falsely reassuring the patient home or reflexively calling 000; provide supportive slow-breathing guidance and clear red-flag safety-netting toward 000.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High in BOTH directions: under-action (accepting 'just anxiety' and discharging home, missing ACS/PE) and over-action (autoimmune-crisis anchoring or reflex 000). The safe telehealth diagnosis is 'probable panic, organic causes not yet excluded'.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Australian acute chest pain assessment principles
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Undifferentiated acute chest pain that cannot be evaluated with examination and ECG warrants timely in-person assessment; anxiety is a diagnosis of exclusion in acute chest pain.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on health anxiety and cyberchondria
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Health anxiety is managed with validation, psychoeducation, reduction of reassurance-seeking and online symptom-checking, breathing/grounding techniques, and psychological therapy (CBT) via a GP mental health treatment plan.
